name: Monitoring Cadence
lever_classification: execution — how to schedule monitoring nights
consequences: Pulling this lever will shift the nightly monitoring schedule from uniform low‑burden recordings to a hybrid model that interleaves full PSG nights on selected participants. This increases data resolution for rare events but raises staffing demands during PSG nights and may extend participant stay duration. The trade‑off is higher event capture probability versus increased operational complexity and cost.
options: Deploy a hybrid monitoring schedule that balances low‑burden nightly recordings with targeted full PSG nights.
Implement a continuous real‑time anomaly detection pipeline that flags potential parasomnia events for immediate technician review.
Adopt a dynamic scheduling algorithm that reallocates PSG nights based on real‑time event density.
review: Hybrid monitoring balances data richness against staffing load; the options overlook how to manage participant fatigue from extended PSG nights.

name: Event‑Detection Architecture
lever_classification: methodology — which event‑detection algorithm to use
consequences: Pulling this lever will replace manual event flagging with a computational detection system that processes multimodal streams in real time. This reduces technician workload and speeds up annotation, but introduces a risk of false positives that could inflate annotation volume and require additional human review. The trade‑off is faster throughput versus potential quality degradation if detection thresholds are not calibrated.
options: Build a rule‑based threshold system that triggers technician alerts when sensor metrics exceed predefined limits.
Train a supervised machine‑learning classifier on annotated PSG data to predict parasomnia episodes from multimodal streams.
Combine unsupervised clustering of sensor patterns with expert‑reviewed anchors to identify novel event signatures.
review: Automated detection promises efficiency but may miss subtle parasomnia signatures; the options do not address calibration against diverse patient phenotypes.

name: Staffing Model
lever_classification: operations — how to structure night‑shift coverage
consequences: Pulling this lever will restructure night‑shift coverage to ensure continuous monitoring across all suites. This improves safety and responsiveness to events but may increase labor costs and require additional training for technicians. The trade‑off is enhanced participant safety versus higher personnel expenses.
options: Expand technician roster to include a dedicated night‑shift specialist who monitors all suites simultaneously.
Rotate a core team of technicians across nights to maintain continuity while sharing expertise.
Introduce a remote monitoring liaison who triages alerts and coordinates on‑site responses.
review: Expanding night coverage improves safety but increases labor costs; the options ignore potential burnout among technicians due to higher shift density.

name: Data‑Sharing Protocol
lever_classification: dissemination — how to share data with external researchers
consequences: Pulling this lever will establish a secure data‑sharing framework that allows external researchers to access de‑identified physiological data. This accelerates scientific collaboration and publication potential but imposes stricter compliance requirements and may delay data release. The trade‑off is broader dissemination versus increased administrative overhead.
options: Release de‑identified physiological datasets through a secure, access‑controlled repository with tiered permissions.
Provide anonymized summary statistics and event logs to collaborators under signed data‑use agreements.
Offer a controlled‑access data portal that requires institutional review board approval before download.
review: Secure data sharing fosters collaboration but adds compliance steps; the options assume all collaborators will meet institutional review board requirements without accounting for varying institutional capacities.

name: Ethics & Consent Flow
lever_classification: governance — how to obtain and manage participant consent
consequences: Pulling this lever will implement a robust consent process that empowers participants to control data sharing levels. This enhances ethical compliance and participant trust but may reduce the volume of usable data for secondary analyses. The trade‑off is higher participant autonomy versus potential data scarcity.
options: Implement an electronic consent platform that records time‑stamped signatures and allows participants to withdraw at any time.
Conduct in‑person consent workshops that walk participants through data‑use scenarios and privacy safeguards.
Provide a tiered consent model where participants choose the level of data sharing they are comfortable with.
review: Tiered consent enhances autonomy but limits data depth; the options fail to consider how reduced data granularity might affect event‑triage algorithm training.

name: Funding Allocation Strategy
lever_classification: governance — how to allocate budget across phases
consequences: Pulling this lever will allocate budget in a phased manner tied to milestone achievements, creating financial safeguards against overruns. This ensures fiscal discipline and protects the project from early cost escalations but may delay procurement of critical equipment if funding is withheld. The trade‑off is risk mitigation versus potential operational delays.
options: Allocate a fixed percentage of the annual budget to contingency reserves to absorb unforeseen renovation costs.
Prioritize early‑stage equipment procurement to lock in favorable pricing before inflation.
Schedule incremental funding releases tied to milestone achievements to maintain financial discipline.
review: Milestone‑based funding protects against overruns but may delay critical purchases; the options do not account for inflationary pressure on equipment prices during the renovation phase.

name: Participant Engagement Pathway
lever_classification: execution — how to recruit and retain participants
consequences: Pulling this lever reshapes how participants are attracted and retained, directly affecting enrollment speed and data quality. A more engaging recruitment strategy can accelerate cohort build‑up but may introduce selection bias if certain demographics are over‑represented. Conversely, a less intensive approach preserves sample representativeness but risks falling short of enrollment targets, delaying downstream analyses.
options: Launch a community outreach program using local sleep clinics and social media, offering educational workshops and incentives for referrals.
Partner with patient advocacy groups to create a peer‑mentoring system where enrolled participants guide new recruits, fostering trust and adherence.
Deploy an interactive web portal that logs sleep diaries, provides personalized feedback, and rewards consistent participation with vouchers.
review: Core tension: speed of recruitment versus data quality; options overlook potential selection bias introduced by peer‑mentoring or digital engagement.

name: Suite Utilization Optimization
lever_classification: operations — how to schedule and allocate sleep suites
consequences: Pulling this lever determines how the eight active suites are scheduled, influencing throughput, staffing load, and participant experience. Optimized scheduling can maximize data capture per night but may compress technician coverage windows, increasing fatigue. Less aggressive utilization preserves staff flexibility but may leave suites idle, raising operational costs.
options: Adopt a staggered admission schedule that aligns participant stays with technician shift patterns, minimizing overnight coverage gaps.
Introduce a dynamic booking algorithm that reallocates suites in real time based on event frequency predictions, ensuring high‑yield participants occupy active suites.
Offer flexible stay extensions for participants with low event capture, reallocating suites to new arrivals during low‑activity periods to maintain utilization.
review: Core tension: maximizing suite occupancy versus participant comfort; options ignore possible noise from overlapping stays.

name: Annotation Workflow Automation
lever_classification: methodology — how to process and score data
consequences: Pulling this lever changes the balance between manual labor and data readiness. Semi‑automated pre‑tagging speeds up annotation but may introduce systematic errors if the algorithm misclassifies events. A fully manual approach guarantees human oversight but delays data availability and increases costs.
options: Deploy a semi‑automated labeling pipeline that pre‑tags suspected events for human review, reducing annotation time by 40%.
Introduce a consensus‑driven double‑scoring system where two independent raters cross‑check each other’s annotations before final approval, enhancing reliability.
Implement a continuous learning feedback loop where scorer corrections are fed back into the algorithm to improve future pre‑tag accuracy.
review: Core tension: speed of annotation versus scorer reliability; options assume equal scorer expertise and ignore fatigue.

name: Risk Mitigation Protocol
lever_classification: governance — how to manage safety and compliance
consequences: Pulling this lever dictates the level of safety vigilance, affecting participant trust and operational flexibility. A high‑alert dashboard can prevent adverse events but may trigger unnecessary interventions, increasing staff workload. A lower‑alert threshold reduces staff burden but risks delayed responses to genuine emergencies.
options: Enforce a real‑time monitoring dashboard that alerts staff to abnormal physiological signals, enabling immediate intervention.
Establish a rapid response team that conducts on‑site safety drills monthly, ensuring readiness for emergencies.
Integrate a predictive analytics model that flags high‑risk participants before admission, allowing pre‑emptive safety measures.
review: Core tension: safety vigilance versus operational flexibility; options fail to account for false positives causing unnecessary interventions.

name: Infrastructure Scalability Plan
consequences: Pulling this lever determines how quickly the unit can grow to meet enrollment targets. Modular expansion preserves the domestic feel but may incur higher per‑suite costs. Repurposing adjacent spaces offers rapid capacity but could compromise privacy. Leasing a satellite site expands reach but introduces regulatory complexity.
options: Design modular suite units that can be added incrementally, allowing expansion to 12 suites without major renovation.
Repurpose adjacent residential spaces into temporary monitoring pods during peak recruitment periods, maintaining a domestic feel.
Lease a nearby commercial building for a satellite monitoring site, enabling parallel data collection while preserving the primary unit’s integrity.
review: Core tension: rapid expansion versus maintaining methodological rigor; options do not consider regulatory hurdles for satellite sites.

name: Regulatory Compliance Cadence
consequences: Pulling this lever shapes the rhythm of ethics and regulatory interactions, influencing project speed and participant trust. Frequent internal audits keep the team prepared but consume resources. A centralized dashboard streamlines documentation but requires upfront development. A dedicated officer accelerates approvals but adds salary cost.
options: Schedule quarterly internal audits that simulate external reviews, ensuring continuous compliance readiness.
Create a centralized compliance dashboard that tracks all documentation, deadlines, and approvals in real time.
Engage a dedicated compliance officer to liaise with ethics boards, streamlining communication and reducing approval delays.
review: Core tension: thorough compliance versus project speed; options assume ethics boards will respond within standard timelines, ignoring potential delays.

name: Participant Retention Strategy
lever_classification: execution — how to keep participants engaged throughout stay
consequences: Implementing a structured retention plan reduces nightly dropout rates, thereby increasing the likelihood of capturing at least one adjudicated event per participant. Enhanced engagement also lowers the proportion of unproductive admissions, keeping the 20% ceiling in check. However, additional support activities may raise per‑participant costs and require extra staff time.
options: Introduce a nightly wellness check that offers personalized sleep hygiene tips and immediate feedback on sensor comfort, encouraging participants to remain in the unit for the full scheduled stay.
Offer a tiered incentive program that rewards participants for completing consecutive nights, with escalating monetary bonuses and complimentary health assessments at the end of the stay.
Deploy a peer‑support network where participants can share anonymized experiences via a secure messaging platform, fostering a sense of community and reducing perceived isolation.
review: The tension between participant comfort and data completeness is addressed, but none of the options explicitly account for the additional staffing burden of nightly wellness checks or the potential for incentive fatigue over longer stays.

name: Night Shift Staffing Flexibility
lever_classification: operations — how to schedule technicians for continuous coverage
consequences: Adopting a flexible shift model allows technicians to respond promptly to escalation nights while maintaining adequate coverage of the 8 active suites. This reduces missed events and improves safety margins. The trade‑off is increased scheduling complexity and potential overtime costs if peak demand periods are misaligned with staff availability.
options: Implement a rotating 12‑hour shift schedule that aligns technician coverage with peak event‑capture windows, ensuring a technician is always on standby during the first, mid‑stay, and final nights.
Introduce a cross‑training program that enables a subset of technicians to perform both routine monitoring and full PSG setup, allowing rapid escalation without hiring additional staff.
Adopt a part‑time night‑shift pool that can be called in during high‑event periods, providing surge capacity while keeping base staffing costs lower.
review: The lever balances coverage and cost, yet the options overlook the impact of shift fatigue on annotation accuracy and the need for formal fatigue mitigation policies.

name: Data Annotation Workflow
lever_classification: execution — how to process annotations efficiently
consequences: Streamlining annotation reduces the backlog of raw sensor data awaiting human scoring, thereby accelerating the release of usable datasets for Aim 2. Faster annotation also shortens the time to publish early results, supporting grant renewal. However, increased automation may introduce subtle biases if not properly validated against human scorers.
options: Deploy a tiered annotation pipeline where technicians first flag potential events using a lightweight rule‑based filter, then senior scorers review only flagged segments, cutting review time by up to 50%.
Introduce a peer‑review system where two technicians independently annotate the same segment and a third resolves discrepancies, ensuring consistency while distributing workload.

Implement a batch‑processing schedule that consolidates nightly data uploads into a single daily annotation session, allowing technicians to focus on high‑priority nights and reducing context switching.
review: The workflow improves efficiency, yet none of the options address how to monitor and correct for systematic annotation drift over the 3‑year period.

name: Ethical Oversight Cadence
lever_classification: governance — how often ethics oversight occurs
consequences: Setting a defined ethics review cadence ensures timely approvals for protocol amendments and participant safety updates, preventing operational delays. Regular oversight also reinforces participant trust and institutional compliance. The downside is that frequent reviews may slow responsiveness to emergent safety signals.
options: Schedule a formal ethics review every six months, with a standing sub‑committee empowered to approve urgent safety changes within 48 hours.
Establish a continuous monitoring dashboard that flags potential ethical concerns in real time, triggering an expedited review process when thresholds are crossed.
Create a joint ethics‑operations task force that meets monthly to pre‑emptively discuss upcoming protocol adjustments and align on risk mitigation strategies.
review: The cadence addresses approval timing, but the options do not consider the resource burden of maintaining a continuous monitoring dashboard or the potential for review fatigue among ethics board members.

name: Equipment Calibration Protocol
lever_classification: operations — how to maintain sensor accuracy
consequences: Regular calibration of dry‑electrode headbands, mattress sensors, and cameras preserves signal quality, directly influencing event detection accuracy. Consistent calibration also reduces false alarm rates, supporting the 20% unproductive admission threshold. The trade‑off is the need for scheduled downtime and additional technical staff time.
options: Implement a weekly calibration routine where each sensor type undergoes a standardized test against a reference signal, with results logged in a central quality‑control database.
Introduce a self‑diagnostic feature in the sensor firmware that alerts technicians to drift, allowing on‑the‑spot recalibration without full shutdown.
Schedule quarterly calibration workshops that bring in external experts to validate sensor performance and update calibration protocols based on emerging best practices.
review: Calibration improves data quality, yet the options fail to address how to handle calibration failures that occur during a participant’s stay without disrupting data collection.

name: Recruitment Funnel Optimization
consequences: Optimizing the recruitment funnel increases the proportion of eligible participants who enroll, directly impacting the 50–70 participant target. A more efficient funnel also reduces the time and cost per enrollment. However, aggressive funnel tactics may raise concerns about participant selection bias.
options: Develop a targeted outreach campaign that partners with regional neurologists to provide pre‑screening webinars, filtering candidates before they reach the study portal.
Create a referral incentive program that rewards current participants for bringing in new eligible volunteers, leveraging peer networks to expand reach.

Implement an automated eligibility screener on the study website that guides potential participants through a structured questionnaire, flagging those who meet inclusion criteria for immediate scheduling.
review: The funnel strategies aim to boost enrollment, but none explicitly consider how to monitor and mitigate selection bias introduced by referral or self‑selection mechanisms.
